commands.review_summary:
The PR composes trusted-tool-policy registrations into the global hook-runner registry, passes that composed registry into before_tool_call diagnostics and enforcement, and adds regression coverage for active/pinned registry drift.

PR surface: Source +65, Tests +155. Total +220 across 6 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level with high confidence: current main composes live registries for hook dispatch while trusted-policy helpers still read the active registry. I did not run tests because this review is read-only.

target.title: fix(memory-core): include path in FTS indexed text so filename tokens are searchable
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix classification: root-cause compatibility repair for memory FTS indexing and legacy FTS schema upgrade.
- Root cause: the schema had no dedicated source-of-truth FTS owner for path tokens, which caused filename/date-token search to fail unless path text was mixed into body indexing and body BM25 ranking.
- Why this is root-cause fix: the patch fixes the schema/source ownership directly: `chunks` remains the canonical chunk store, `chunks_fts` indexes body text only for BM25, and `chunks_fts_path` indexes path text only for filename/date-token lookup.
- Why it matters / User impact: users searching memory by a file timestamp or filename token such as `2026-06-17-1649` should find the memory entry without making unrelated body matches rank worse.
- What did NOT change / scope boundary: this changes only derived FTS indexing/search behavior; it does not change vector search behavior, memory file format, provider behavior, auth behavior, or broad memory API contract.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: schema setup owns FTS table creation and legacy rebuild; search owns merging body and path keyword results; embedding/sync cleanup owns keeping both FTS tables consistent with `chunks`.
- Patch quality notes: the diff is larger than the original XS path-prefix patch because it removes the artificial path prefix, adds a separate FTS path table, and updates all write/delete/reset paths so the schema remains internally consistent.
- Related open PR scan: this is an update to existing PR #94114 for linked issue #94102; no separate new competing PR is being opened by this flow.
- Out of scope: changing vector search behavior, changing memory file formats, or broadening memory schema beyond the FTS path index repair.

## Linked context

Closes #94102

Related #94114

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: memory search should find indexed memory files by filename/date tokens without weakening body-text relevance ranking.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw memory-core manager tests using the real memory manager sync/search path and SQLite FTS5 tables.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: focused `manager-filename-token` validation ran the manager-level filename token regression after indexing a memory file named `2026-06-17-1649.md`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied live terminal output after the fix:

  ```text
   RUN  v4.1.8 [local path redacted]
  
  
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  1 passed | 51 skipped (52)
     Start at  12:47:57
     Duration  18.70s (transform 12.08s, setup 785ms, import 17.00s, tests 449ms, environment 0ms)
  ```

- Observed result after fix: after `manager.sync({ reason: "test" })`, `manager.search("2026-06-17-1649", { maxResults: 3 })` returns `memory/2026-06-17-1649.md`.
- What was not tested: the full extension-memory Vitest shard did not complete in the local harness because it was terminated after 120 seconds with no output; focused memory search/indexing coverage was run instead.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: proof uses the real manager sync/search code path in tests rather than an interactive gateway session.

## Tests and validation

- Target test file: `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts` and `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts` cover the FTS ranking, legacy rebuild, and manager sync/search behavior.
- Scenario locked in: filename/date-token search finds `memory/2026-06-17-1649.md` after indexing, while body BM25 ordering stays stable when paths are indexed separately.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: targeted memory tests exercise the changed schema/search/write paths directly; the full extension-memory shard was attempted but the local harness terminated it after 120 seconds with no output.
- `manager-search`: 26 tests passed.
- `manager-filename-token`: 1 passed, 51 skipped.
- `fts-state`: 2 tests passed.
- `hybrid-memory`: 5 tests passed.
- `embedding-cache`: 3 tests passed.
- `format-memory-fts-files`: all matched files use correct format.
- `diff-check`: passed.

Regression coverage added or updated:

- Body BM25 ordering stays stable when long paths are indexed separately.
- Filename/date tokens match through the separate path FTS table.
- Legacy FTS tables rebuild from existing chunks so old memory rows become filename-searchable.
- Legacy databases that are missing `chunks.source` or old FTS `source` columns rebuild safely.
- Manager-level sync/search confirms filename token search works after indexing a real memory file.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

What is the highest-risk area?

Existing local memory SQLite stores may have older FTS schemas.

How is that risk mitigated?

The schema setup detects old or incomplete FTS tables, recreates only the FTS tables, and backfills them from the canonical `chunks` table. Body-text FTS stays separate from path FTS so filename search does not change body BM25 document-length normalization.

- Risk labels considered: compatibility and memory indexing/session-state behavior.
- Risk explanation: existing stores can have legacy FTS tables, so the compatibility risk is whether old chunks become searchable without corrupting canonical chunk data.
- Why acceptable: the migration only rebuilds derived FTS tables from canonical `chunks`; it does not rewrite memory files, vectors, auth/config, or provider state.

## Current review state

- Maintainer-ready confidence: high after focused validations and PR body proof updates; the remaining remote state to observe is normal PR settle after update.
- Next action: ready for maintainer review after the PR update settles.
- Waiting on author: no known code or proof follow-up after the focused validations above.
- RF-001 (`triage: needs-real-behavior-proof`): addressed with copied terminal output from the manager-level filename-token regression after indexing.
- RF-002 (add payload/version or migration): addressed by schema detection that rebuilds old or incomplete FTS tables and backfills from `chunks`.
- RF-003 (stronger real behavior proof): addressed by running the patched memory manager sync/search path instead of only an in-memory FTS insert simulation.
- RF-004 (old clean indexes stay body-only): addressed by adding the separate path FTS table and legacy rebuild coverage for existing chunks.
- RF-005 (snippet/scoring consumes artificial path prefix): addressed by removing the path-prepended body text approach; returned text/snippets come from canonical chunk text.
- RF-006 (posted proof was only simulation/dist grep): addressed by the `manager.sync()` plus `manager.search("2026-06-17-1649")` regression proof.
- RF-007 (repair lane needs compatibility, snippet cleanup, proof): addressed by the legacy rebuild path, removal of artificial path prefixes, and updated proof above.
- RF-008 (trigger reindex for new FTS payload): addressed by rebuilding missing/legacy FTS schemas from the canonical chunks table.
- RF-009 (strip path prefix before snippets): stale after this update because the implementation no longer prepends paths to indexed body text.
- Addressed feedback: replaced the path-prepended FTS approach with separate body/path FTS tables, added legacy reindex coverage, and added ranking non-regression coverage.
target.author: zhangguiping-xydt
